question: A hollow charged metal sphere has radius ${r}$. If the potential difference between its surface and a point at distance ${3 r}$ from the centre is ${V}$, then the electric intensity at distance ${3 r}$ from the centre is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950230/preptracker/questions/q_41516_explanation_1764950229645.png $V=V_{s}-V_{p}$
${=\dfrac{k Q}{r}-\dfrac{k Q}{3 r}}$
${=\dfrac{2 k Q}{3 r}}$
${E=\dfrac{k Q}{(3 r)^{2}}}$
${=\dfrac{V}{6 r}}$

question:
Read the and carefully to mark correct options given below:

Assertion: Work done by electric field on moving a positive charge on an equipotential surface is always zero.

Reason: Electric lines of forces are always perpendicular to equipotential surfaces.
explanation:
Work done by any conservative force is given by
${W_C} = \Delta U = q\Delta V$
${\rm{Where}}\,\,\Delta U = {\rm{Change}}\,\,{\rm{in}}\,\,{\rm{electric}}\,\,{\rm{potential}}\,\,{\rm{energy}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\Delta V = {\rm{Change}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{in}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{electric}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{potential}}$
So, correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Equipotential Surfaces

question:

Reason: Electric field is perpendicular to equipotential surface always.
explanation: Electric field lines on a conductor are perpendicular to its surface as, at equilibrium, the electric field inside is zero, and lines terminate on the surface, aligning perpendicularly. This illustrates the relationship between electric field lines and equipotential surfaces. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: At a point in space, the electric field points towards north. In the region, surrounding this point the rate of change of potential will be zero along the east and west.

Reason: Electric field can be parallel to equipotential surface.
explanation:
If the electric field points towards the north at a point in space, the electric potential (voltage) will not change along the east-west direction because electric field lines are perpendicular to $E P S$ (equipotential surfaces).
'Equipotential' $\Rightarrow \Delta V=0$
$V=$ constant https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956210/preptracker/questions/q_41284_explanation_1764956209729.png $\Rightarrow E(\Delta r) \cos \theta=0$$\Rightarrow \theta=90^{\circ}$
So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Surface of a symmetrical conductor can be treated as equipotential surface.

Reason: Charges can easily flow in a conductor.
explanation: In a symmetrical conductor, the charges distribute themselves in such a way that the electric potential is the same at all points on its surface, making it an equipotential surface. The reason is valid because charges shall easily move within a conductor to make $\Delta V=0$ on surface So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Equipotential Surfaces

question:
Assertion: Electric field is always directed perpendicular to an equipotential surface.

Reason: Equipotential surface is a surface on which at each point potential is same.
explanation:
$E=\dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta r}($ or $) \Delta V=\vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{\Delta r}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956202/preptracker/questions/q_41216_explanation_1764956202029.png $\Delta V=E(\Delta r) \cos \theta$
For $(E P S), \Delta V=0 \Rightarrow \theta=90^{\circ}$. An equipotential surface $(E P S)$ is defined as a surface where the electric potential is constant at every point. The electric field lines are always perpendicular to equipotential surfaces. This is because if there were an electric field component parallel to the surface, there would be a potential difference along the surface, which would violate the definition of an equipotential surface. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Equipotential Surfaces

question: Equipotential surfaces are shown in figure. Then the electric field strength will be
explanation:
Using $d V=-\vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}$
$\Rightarrow \Delta V=-E \Delta r \cos \theta$
$\Rightarrow E=\dfrac{-\Delta V}{\Delta r \cos \theta}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950161/preptracker/questions/q_41126_explanation_1764950160917.jpg $\Rightarrow E=\dfrac{-(20-10)}{10 \times 10^{-2} \cos 120^{\circ}}$
$=\dfrac{-10}{10 \times 10^{-2}\left(-\sin 30^{\circ}\right)}$
$ = 200\,V{\rm{/}}m$
Direction of $\vec E$ shall be from higher potential surface towards lower potential surface. Direction of $E$ be perpendicular to the equipotential surface i.e., at $\left( {90^\circ + 30^\circ } \right) = 120^\circ $ with $X$-axis.
So, option (3) is correct.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Relation between Electric Field and Electric Potential

question: There is a uniform electrostatic field in a region.The potential at various points on a small sphere centred at $p$, in the region, is found to vary between in limits 589.0 $V$ to 589.8 $V$. What is the potential at a point on the sphere whose radius vector makes an angle of  $60^\circ $ with the direction of the field ?
explanation:
The potential difference across the sphere of diameter 2 $R$ is 0.8 $V$. The potential at a point $P$ whose radius vector makes an angle $60^\circ $ is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956163/preptracker/questions/q_40868_explanation_1764956163391.png The potential difference between $A$ and $P$ is
${V_A} - {V_P} = \left( {\frac{{\Delta V}}{{2R}}} \right)\left( {R + \frac{R}{2}} \right) = \frac{3}{4}\Delta V = \frac{3}{4} \times 0.8V$
${V_A} - {V_P} = 0.6V,\,\,\,{V_P} = 589.2V$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Relation between Electric Field and Electric Potential

question: An electric field  $\overrightarrow E  = (25\hat i + 30\hat j)N{C^{ - 1}}$ exists in a region of space. If the potential at the origin is taken to be zero then the potential at $x = 2\,m,y = 2m$ is:
explanation:
The potential difference between two points is
${V_2} - {V_1} = - \int\limits_1^2 {\bar E.d\bar r} $
$V - 0 = - \left[ {\int\limits_0^2 {{E_{\rm{x}}}d{\rm{x}}} + \int\limits_0^2 {{E_{\rm{y}}}d{\rm{y}}} } \right]$
$V = - \left[ {25\left[ x \right]_0^2 + 30\left[ y \right]_0^2} \right]$
$V = - \left[ {50 + 60} \right] = - 110V$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Relation between Electric Field and Electric Potential

question: A field of \[100\,V{m^{ - 1}}\] is directed at \[{30^o}\] to positive \[x\]-axis. Find \[{V_A} - {V_B}\] if \[OA = 2\,m\] and \[OB = 4\,m\]
explanation:
We know that
${V_2} - {V_1} = - \int_1^2 {\overline E \,.d\overline r } $
Given that $\overline E = 50\sqrt 3 \,\hat i + 50\,\hat j$
${V_A} - {V_0} = - \int\limits_{{\rm{x}} = 0}^{{\rm{x}} = 2} {\overline E \,.d\overline r } = - {E_{\rm{x}}}\,\Delta {\rm{x}} = - 100\sqrt 3 $
${V_B} - {V_0} = - \int\limits_{y = 0}^{y = 4} {\overline E \,.d\overline r } = - {E_y}\,\Delta y = - 200$
${V_B} - {V_A} = 100\left( {\sqrt 3 - 2} \right)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Relation between Electric Field and Electric Potential

question: Charges are placed on the vertices of a square as shown. Let $E$ be electric field and $V$ the potential at the centre. If the charges on $A$ and $B$ are interchanged with those on $D$ and $C$, respectively then
explanation: $V$ remains unchanged, because with the change in position of charges, distance $AO$, $BO$, $CO$, and $DO$ remain same. The direction of  $\overrightarrow {\rm{E}} $ changes.  https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956162/preptracker/questions/q_40840_explanation_1764956161747.png

question: The electrostatic potential inside a charged spherical ball is given by  $\phi  = a{r^2} + b$ where $r$ is the distance from the centre; $a,b$ are constants. Then the charge density inside the ball is
explanation:
$\phi = a{r^2} + b \Rightarrow E = - \frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}} = - 2ar$
From Gauss law
$\oint {\overrightarrow E .d\overrightarrow S } = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}} \Rightarrow - 2ar.4\pi {r^2} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}} \Rightarrow q = - 8{\varepsilon _0}a\pi {r^3}$
$\rho = \frac{q}{{\frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3}}} \Rightarrow \rho = - 6a{\varepsilon _0}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Relation between Potential and Charge Density

question: The electric potential at a point (x,y,z) is given by $V =  - {x^2}y - x{z^3} + 4$ The electric field $E$ at the point is
explanation:
The electric field related with potential is
$E = - \frac{{\partial V}}{{\partial x}}\hat i - \frac{{\partial V}}{{\partial y}}\hat j - \frac{{\partial V}}{{\partial z}}\hat k$
As $V = - {x^2}y - x{z^3} + 4$
$E = (2xy + {z^3})\hat i + {x^2}\hat j + 3x{z^2}\hat k$
subject: Physics

question: The potential of the electric field produced by a point charge at any point (x, y, z) is given by $V = 3{x^2} + 5$, where x, y, z are in metres and V is in volts. The intensity of the electric field at $( - 2,1,0)$ is
explanation:
As potential is independent of $y$ and $z$, electric field components in those two directions are zero.
Since $V = 3{x^2} + 5$
$E = - \,\,\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = - \frac{d}{{dx}}(3{x^2} + 5) = - 6x$
Intensity of the electic field at $x = - 2$
$ = - 6( - 2) = + 12\,V{m^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Relation between Electric Field and Electric Potential

question: The electric potential at any point $x,y,z$ in  metres is given by $V = 3{x^2}$. The electric field at a point $(2m,0,1\,m)$ is
explanation:
Here, electric potential $V = 3{x^2}$
Since potential is independent of $y$ and $z$, electric field components in those two directions are zero. Hence
$E = - \frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = - \frac{d}{{dx}}(3{x^2}) = - 6x$
${E_{(2m.0m.1m)}} = - 12\,V\,{m^{ - 1}}$

question: The electric potential at a point in free space  due to a charge $Q$ coulomb is  $Q \times {10^{11}}V.$ The electric field at the point is
explanation:
As potential at a point due to a point charge is
$V = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \times \frac{Q}{r}$
The electric field is
$E = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \times \frac{Q}{{{r^2}}}$
Given that $V = Q \times {10^{11}}$
From the above equations
$E = 4\pi {\varepsilon _o}Q \times {10^{22}}Vm$
subject: Physics

question: The work done to move a charge on an  equipotential surface is
explanation:

Thus, the work done to move a charge q on it is $W = q\,\Delta \,V = 0$

question: In a region, the potential is represented by  $V(x,y,z) = 6x - 8xy - 8y + 6yz,$ where $V$ is in   volts and $x,y,z$ are in metres. The electric forces  experienced by a charge of 2$C$ situated at point (1,1,1) is
explanation:
As we know that
${E_x} = \frac{{ - \partial V}}{{\partial x}},{E_y} = \frac{{ - \partial V}}{{\partial y}}\& {E_z} = \frac{{ - \partial V}}{{\partial z}}$
As $V = 6x - 8xy - 8y + 6yz$
$\bar E = - [(6 - 8y)\hat i + ( - 8x - 8 + 6z)\hat j + 6y\hat k]$
The value of E at coordinate (1,1,1)
$E = - [ - 2\hat i - 10\hat j + 6\hat k]$
So,${E_{net}} = \sqrt {{{(2)}^2} + {{(10)}^2} + {{( - 6)}^2}} = 2\sqrt {35} \,NC$
and force on charge q due to ${E_{net}}$ is given by
$F = q\,{E_{net}} = 2 \times 2\sqrt {35} = 4\sqrt {35} \,N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Relation between Electric Field and Electric Potential

question: The figure gives the  electric potential $V$ as a function of distance through  four regions on $X$-axis. Which of the following is true for the magnitude of the electric field $E$ in these regions?
explanation:
$E = - \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}\, = - $ (slope of the curve)
In region $A$ and $C$ slope is zero.
So,${E_A} = {E_C} = 0$
In region $B$, ${E_B} = - {\left. {\frac{{dV}}{{dx}}} \right|_B}\, = \, - \frac{{4\, - \,2}}{{2\, - \,1}}\, = \, - 2$
In region D, ${E_D} = - {\left. {\frac{{dV}}{{dx}}} \right|_D}\, = \, - \frac{{0\, - \,4}}{{5\, - \,4}}\, = \,\,4$
$\therefore \,{E_B} < {E_D}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Relation between Electric Field and Electric Potential

question: Figure shows three  points $A$, $B$ and $C$ in a region of uniform  electric field $E$. The line  $AB$ is perpendicular and $BC$ is parallel to the field lines. Then which of the following holds good? (${V_A},{V_B}\,{\rm{and}}\,{V_C}$ represent the electric potential at points $A$, $B$ and $C$ respectively)
explanation:
The plane perpendicular to the field lines in which the points $A$ and $B$ lies can be considered as an equipotential surface.
$\therefore \,{V_A} = {V_B}$
Since the field lines are always directed from higher potential to lower potential,
$\therefore \,{V_B} > {V_C}$
subject: Physics

question:
The diagram below show regions of equipotentials :-

question:
I. At the surface of a charged conductor, electrostatic field must be normal to the surface at everypoint
II. The interior of a solid conductor can have no charge in static situation.
III. For a uniform electric field along Y-axis the equipotential surfaces will be in xy plane.
explanation: If there is a component of electric field  parallel to the surface then charge will experience force and it starts moving on the surface, so electric field should be always  perpendicular to the surface in electrostatic situation. If there is any charge inside a conductor then it should flows to the outer surface because of repulsion,  so no charge resides on outer surface. If the  electric field is along $y$ - axis then equipotential surfaces will be along $xz$ - plane.

options.text: I is correct, II is incorrect

question: The potential at a point $x$ (measured in $\mu $ $m$) due to some charges situated on the $x$-axis is given by  $V\left( x \right) = \frac{{20}}{{{x^2} - 4}}$ volt. The electric field $E$ at $x = 4\,\mu $ $m$ is given by
explanation:
The $x$ - component of electric field is
${E_x} = \frac{{ - dV}}{{dx}} = - 20\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\frac{1}{{{x^2} - 4}}} \right)$
${E_x} = \frac{{40x}}{{\left( {{x^2} - 4} \right)}} \Rightarrow {E_x} = \frac{{10}}{9}\frac{V}{{\mu m}}\left( {{\mathop{\rm at}\nolimits} {\mkern 1mu} \,x = 4\mu m} \right)$

options.text: $\frac{5}{3}\frac{V}{{\mu m}}$ and in the positive $x$ - direction
$\frac{{10}}{9}\frac{V}{{\mu m}}$ and in the negative $x$ - direction
$\frac{5}{3}\frac{V}{{\mu m}}$ and in the negative direction

question: The electric potential  $V$ at any point $({\rm{x}},y,z)$,  all in metres in space is given by  $V = 4{x^2}$ volt. The electric field at the point (1, 0, 2) in volt/meter, is
explanation:
$\overrightarrow E = - \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}\hat i = - 8x\hat i$ volt/meter
${E_{(1,0,2)}} = - 8i\, V/m$

question: At a certain distance from a point charge the  electric field is 500 $V$/$m$ and the potential is  3000 $V$. What is this distance?
explanation:
The relation between $V$ and $E$ by a point charge is
$V = E \times r \Rightarrow r = \frac{V}{E} = \frac{{3000}}{{500}} = 6m$

question: A uniform electric field of magnitude $E$ and directed along positive $x$-axis exists in a certain region of space. At  $x = 0$ the electric potential $V$ is zero, then the potential at $x =  + {x_0}$ is
explanation:
$E = E\hat i$
$\int_0^V {dV} = - \int_0^{{x_0}} {Edx} $
$V = - E{x_0}$

question: The potential of the electric field produced by point charge at any point ($x$, $y$, $z$) is given by  $ - 2{x^2}$ where $x$, $y$ are in metre and $V$ is in volts.  The intensity of the electric field at $( - 2,\,1,\,0)$ is :
explanation:
Given that
$V = - 2{x^2}$
Intensity of the electric field
${E_x} = \frac{{ - dV}}{{dx}} = 4x = 4\left( { - 2} \right)$ $(E\,at\,x = - 2)$
$ = - 8V{m^{ - 1}}$

question: There is a uniform electric field of intensity $A$ which is as shown. How many labelled points have the same electric potential as the fully shaded point?
explanation:

question: The potential field of an electric field $\overrightarrow E  = (y\hat i + x\hat j)$ is
explanation:
The potential difference is
$dV = - \overrightarrow E .d\overrightarrow r $
$ = - (y\hat i + {\rm{x}}\hat j).(d{\rm{x}}\hat i + dy\hat j)$
$ = - (yd{\rm{x}} + {\rm{x}}dy) = - d({\rm{x}}y)$
Integrating, we get
$V = - {\rm{x}}y + {\rm{constant}}$

question: Electric potential at any point is $V =  - 5x + 3y + \sqrt {15} \,z,$  then the magnitude of  the electric field is :
explanation:
${E_x} = - \frac{{\partial V}}{{\partial x}} = - \left( { - 5} \right) = 5;{E_y} = - \frac{{\partial V}}{{\partial y}} = - 3$
and ${E_z} = - \frac{{\partial V}}{{\partial z}} = - \sqrt {15} $
${E_{net}} = \sqrt {E_x^2 + E_y^2 + E_z^2} $
$ = \sqrt {{{\left( 5 \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 3} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - \sqrt {15} } \right)}^2}} = 7$
subject: Physics

question: A uniform electric field having a magnitude  ${E_0}$ and direction along the positive $X$-axis exists . If the potential $V$ is zero at $x = 0$ , then its value at $X =  + x$ will be:
explanation:

question: Assume that an electric field $\overrightarrow E  = 30{{\rm{x}}^2}\hat i$ exists in space. Then the potential difference ${V_A} - {V_O}$, where ${V_O}$ is the potential at the origin and  ${V_A}$ the potential at ${\rm{x = }}2{\rm{m}}$  is
explanation:
The potential difference between $A$ and $O$ is
${{\rm{V}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{V}}_{\rm{O}}}{\rm{ = - }}\int_{\rm{O}}^{\rm{A}} {{{\rm{E}}_{\rm{x}}}} {\rm{dx}}$
${V_A} - {V_O} = - \int_0^2 {30{x^2}} dx$
$ = - 30\frac{{{2^3}}}{3} = - 80V$

question: The electric potential at a point $({\rm{x}},y,z)$  is given by  $V =  - {{\rm{x}}^2}y - {\rm{x}}{z^3} + 4$ The electric field  $\overrightarrow E $ at that  point is
explanation:
${E_x} = - \frac{{dV}}{{d{\rm{x}}}} = - \left[ { - 2{\rm{x}}y - {z^3}} \right]$
${E_y} = - \frac{{dV}}{{dy}} = - \left[ { - {{\rm{x}}^2}} \right]$
${E_z} = - \frac{{dV}}{{dz}} = - \left[ { - 3{\rm{x}}{{\rm{z}}^2}} \right]$
$\therefore \overrightarrow E = {E_{\rm{x}}}\hat i + {E_y}\hat j + {E_z}\hat k = (2{\rm{x}}y + {z^3})\hat i + {{\rm{x}}^2}\hat j + 3{\rm{x}}{z^2}\hat k$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Relation between Electric Field and Electric Potential

question: An electric field is expressed as $\overrightarrow E  = 2\widehat i + 3\widehat j$. Find the potential difference $({V_A} - {V_B})$ between two points $A$ and $B$ whose position vectors are given by ${r_A} = \widehat i + 2\widehat j$ and ${r_B} = 2\widehat i + \widehat j + 3\widehat k$
explanation:
We know that
${V_2} - {V_1} = - \int\limits_1^2 {\vec E \cdot d\vec r} $
$ = - \left[ {\int\limits_{{x_1}}^{{x_2}} {{E_x}dx} + \int\limits_{{y_1}}^{{y_2}} {{E_y}dy} } \right]$
${V_B} - {V_A} = - \left[ {\int\limits_1^2 {2dx} + \int\limits_2^1 {3dy} } \right]$
$\, = - \left[ {2(2 - 1) + 3(1 - 2)} \right]$
$ = - [2 - 3] = 1V$
${\rm{Hence}},\,\,{V_A} - {V_B} = - 1V$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Relation between Electric Field and Electric Potential

question: In a Van de Graaff type generator, a spherical  metal shell is to be at a $1.5 \times {10^6}V$. The  dielectric stregth of the gas surrounding the  electrode is $3 \times {10^6}V{m^{ - 1}}$. What is minimum  radius of the spherical shell required?
explanation:
Maximum permissible field strength,
$E = 3 \times {10^6}V{m^{ - 1}}$
Potential on the surface of the sphere
$ = 1.5 \times {10^6}V$
In case of charged spherical cell electric field ($E$) and electric potential ($V$) at a point on its surface is given by
$E = K\,q{\rm{/}}{r^2}$ and $V = K\,q{\rm{/}}r$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,r = V{\rm{/}}E$
When $E$ is maximum, $r$ is minimum
$r = \frac{{1.5 \times {{10}^6}V}}{{3 \times {{10}^6}V{\rm{/}}m}} = 0.5\,m = 50\,cm$
